summary: McKenzie recounts a brief cautionary tale about accidentally overwriting critical data files by using the wrong mv command variant during deployment preparation. The post argues that even solo developers should use source control, illustrated by his own mistake of confusing two similar but functionally different shell commands. He notes the incident cost him three hours but expresses confidence in still meeting his launch deadline thanks to schedule slack.
key_points: A subtle difference between mv commands can clobber existing files in a deployment directory
Even solo developers need source control as a safety net against their own mistakes
Building slack into project schedules provides crucial buffer for recovering from unexpected setbacks
money_quotes: One of puts some critical data files into the directory I'm about to zip for deployment. The other puts some critical data files into the directory I'm about to zip for deployment… and clobbers the identically named data files already there.
Can you spot the difference? This addled Windows programmer did about two seconds too late.
Yay for building some slack into the schedule.
key_insight: Source control is essential even for solo developers because humans inevitably make destructive mistakes, and version history is your cheapest insurance policy.
